title: Dungeon Universalis - A look at the upgrade pack
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TP7VDtErEvY

Two years on from the original review, Daniel (@dungeondive) opens the **Dungeon Universalis** upgrade pack and finds Ludic Dragon has done the work he asked for in 2020.

The headline upgrade is the **revised rulebook** — 124 pages, separating the AI dark-player rules into their own section and tightening the prose throughout. Daniel quotes the same combat-turn paragraph he held up as Exhibit A in the original review and shows it now reads cleanly. A bundled **basic-rules book** (138 pages, simplified, fully campaign-compatible) lowers the on-ramp for new players.

**Campaign & Quest Book 2** adds a 21-quest main campaign (The Night Stone), four smaller campaigns, and standalone quests, all mix-and-matchable with the original quest book — the campaign map turns into an open-world quest-pickup space. A separate small Snake Island Oracle campaign brings naval/pirate flavour. The map itself has been redrawn — better colour, no longer reads as one big desert. Counters are now double-sided, fixing the original's biggest cardboard complaint. Two decks of replacement cards correct or rebalance original components.

But the standout addition is the **Bestiary Cards** (192 cards, sold separately from the upgrade pack but Daniel's strong recommendation). One card per enemy, *with all stats, weapons and skills baked in* — no more cross-referencing weapon decks and skill decks during combat. Twelve faction sub-decks plus unaffiliated monsters and beasts. "I wish more dungeon crawls had bestiary cards like this."

----

Daniel calls baked-in-stats bestiary cards the upgrade that fundamentally improves the play experience — combat speeds up because nothing is cross-referenced. Why do you think this isn't the default in dungeon-crawl design?
